title: Diarrhoea
causes: Bacterial: Salmonella spp., Shigella, Campylobacter, E. coli, Vibrio cholerae

severe: Skin pinch goes back very slowly

dehydrationTable.none: Well, alert, drinks normally, skin goes back quickly
dehydrationTable.some: Restless/irritable, thirsty, sunken eyes, skin goes back slowly
dehydrationTable.severe: Lethargic/unconscious, drinks poorly, very sunken eyes, skin goes back very slowly

diagnosticClues: Diarrhoea WITH vomiting, low grade fever, no mucus → viral
Diarrhoea WITH vomiting, fever, cramps, blood/mucus → bacterial
Diarrhoea WITH blood/mucus WITHOUT fever → amoebiasis
Diarrhoea + oral/skin lesions, weight loss → HIV
Diarrhoea alternating with constipation in adults → bowel malignancy

A.name: Home Treatment - No Dehydration
instructions: Give home-based fluids: rice water, koko, soup, ORS
Give as much fluid as child wants
Return if worsens or not better in 2 days

ORS.preparation: Dissolve sachet in 600ml or 1000ml water (check type)

B.name: Facility Treatment - Some Dehydration
instructions: If vomits, wait 10 minutes and restart
B.monitoring: Reassess after 4 hours: if improved → Plan A; if still some → repeat Plan B; if worse → Plan C

C.name: Emergency Treatment - Severe Dehydration
instructions: If can drink, give ORS while setting IV
When drinking well and improving → Plan B
C.monitoring: Assess infant after 6 hours, child after 3 hours

firstLine.indication: Amoebic dysentery (failing empirical treatment in 2 days or stool microscopy)

medications.reason: Should not be used in children - do more harm than good

drugs: Antibiotic preparations with kaolin or pectin
medications.reason: No therapeutic value in diarrhoea management

referralCriteria: Poor response to rehydration (passing more stools than drinking)

measures: Hand washing with soap after toilet

vaccination.schedule: 2 doses at 6-10 weeks and 14 weeks (2nd dose by 16 weeks, not later than 24 weeks)
vaccination.note: Part of routine immunization in Ghana

nhisBilling.typicalLOS: 3-5 days for severe cases requiring admission
nhisBilling.billingNotes: For inpatient management with dehydration. OPD cases use general OPD tariff.

title: Immunisation

vaccineComposition.fiveInOne: Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus, Haemophilus Influenzae B, Hepatitis B

vaccineUse: Wrong method of administration reduces efficacy
If Immunisation course interrupted, resume with same brand, else restart
No contraindications for sick child if well enough to go home
Children with diarrhoea due for oral vaccines: give but don't count, then repeat after 4 weeks
Don't give OPV 0 after 15 days (disturbs regular schedule)
Don't give Rotarix 1st dose after 12 weeks, 2nd dose after 24 weeks
pretermBabies: Consider all premature babies for full immunisation course
Start immunisation in 2nd month after birth (after 4 weeks) irrespective of prematurity
Delay OPV in neonatal unit, give on discharge

contraindications.condition: History of severe reaction after previous dose (anaphylaxis)
Convulsion/shock within 3 days of most recent DPT dose
Recurrent convulsions or active CNS disease

contraindications.action: No BCG or Yellow Fever (live vaccines). BCG can be given at birth as asymptomatic
No live vaccines; defer 6 months after stopping chemotherapy

exception: Measles vaccine SHOULD be given to HIV positive patients (benefit outweighs risk)
referralCriteria: Refer all problems related to Immunisation to a paediatrician or EPI specialist

nhisBilling.billingNotes: Immunisation is free through EPI; NHIS covers consultation costs only

title: Pertussis

nonPharmacological: Isolation for 5 days after start of antibiotics

secondLine.dose: Apply over whole body after bath at night (except face and head), wash off next morning
secondLine.note: Children >2y use 25% half-strength dilution. Treat all contact cases simultaneously

secondLine.dose: Apply to whole body, leave 24 hours, wash off
secondLine.note: Not recommended <6 months. Clothing and bed linen changed and washed daily with disinfectant, ironed or dry-cleaned

atopicEczema.dose: Use for bathing 12 hourly before applying cream

pediculosis.dose: Apply to whole body, leave 24 hours, wash off
pediculosis.note: Decontaminate combs, hats, clothing, bedding by washing, ironing or fumigation

title: Urticaria
definition: Transient, itchy swelling of skin secondary to histamine release

physical: Cholinergic urticaria (sweating, exercise, emotions, hot foods)

investigations.name: Usually none
investigations.note: FBC may show eosinophilia (allergy/parasites) or low WBC (SLE)

nonPharmacological: Avoid contact with suspected allergen/causative agent

childDose.10-18y: 12.5-25 mg 8-12 hourly or 25 mg at night
childDose.5-10y: 6.25-12.5 mg 12 hourly or 10-25 mg at night
childDose.2-5y: 6.25 mg 12 hourly or 5-15 mg at night

title: Reactive Erythema and Bullous Reaction

types.erythemaMultiforme: Itchy, target-like, non-scaly lesions of palms, soles, forearms, legs
types.stevensJohnsonSyndrome: Erythema and blister formation with mucous membrane involvement (conjunctiva, mouth, genitals)
types.toxicEpidermalNecrolysis: Generalized scalded skin reaction, often drug-induced

causes: Viral infections: herpes simplex, retrovirus, CMV
Adverse drug reactions: sulphonamides, penicillin, NSAIDs, anticonvulsants
signs: Nikolsky sign (skin shears with lateral pressure)

objectives: Prevent further exposure to causative agent/drug
nonPharmacological: Prevent contact of ulcerated skin with contaminated linen (nursing cradle)

unableToSwallow.adultDose: 100 mg 6 hourly until able to swallow
unableToSwallow.childDose: 4 mg/kg 6 hourly until able to swallow

skinCare.note: Do not use silver sulfadiazine (sulfa drug)

referralCriteria: All patients to appropriate specialist (dermatology, ophthalmology, burns unit)

title: Acne Vulgaris
causes: Increased sensitivity of sebaceous glands to androgens
Contraceptive agents (medroxyprogesterone, implants, IUD, oral contraceptives)
symptoms: Pimples on face, trunk, chest, shoulders

investigations.name: Usually none
investigations.note: Serum testosterone in females with hirsutism/virilising features

investigations.name: Pelvic ultrasound
investigations.indication: Females with hirsutism to exclude polycystic ovaries

topical.caution: Contraindicated in pregnancy. Avoid sun, eyes, nostrils, mouth, mucous membrane, broken skin

topical.dose: Apply thinly once daily at night

oral.note: Change to erythromycin after 4 months if poor response

causes.atopic: Genetic, often familial history of atopy (asthma, hay fever, eosinophilia, allergic rhinitis)
causes.contact: Irritants (acids, alkalis, detergents, petroleum products); Allergens (nickel, rubber, chromates, hair dyes, topical medicaments, plants)
causes.varicose: Venous stasis (varicose veins, heart failure, lymphoedema)

antihistamines.adultDose: 4 mg 4-6 hourly or 4 mg at night

childDose.6-12y: 2 mg 4-6 hourly or 2 mg at night
childDose.2-6y: 1 mg 4-6 hourly or 1 mg at night
childDose.1-2y: 1 mg 12 hourly or 1 mg at night

shampoos.dose: Apply twice weekly for 4 weeks with ≥3 days between applications
Apply 2 times/week for 2 weeks, then 1-2 weekly for control, leave 2-3 min then rinse

oral.caution: Potentially life-threatening liver toxicity - monitor LFTs
Potentially life-threatening liver toxicity - monitor LFTs

title: Diabetes Mellitus

causes.type2: Genetic factors affecting insulin action/secretion, environmental factors (excess calories, lack of physical activity)
causes.secondary: Corticosteroid use, pancreatic disease, pancreatectomy, Cushing's syndrome, acromegaly

objectives: Achieve glycaemic targets: FBG 4-7 mmol/L, 2h post-meal 5-9 mmol/L, HbA1c ≤7%

diet: Avoid refined sugars (soft drinks, added sugar)
Children: dietician consultation for optimal growth

alcohol.adult: Low consumption permissible; moderate to heavy increases caloric intake, worsens obesity, increases hypoglycaemia risk

type1.note: Insulin dose: 0.6-0.8 units/kg/day in divided doses. Start lower and build up.

regimens.name: Twice daily pre-mixed insulin (preferred)
regimens.dose: ⅔ total daily dose before breakfast, ⅓ before supper

regimens.name: Soluble + NPH twice daily
regimens.route: SC 30 min before breakfast and supper

monotherapy.adultDose: 500 mg-1 g daily, increase every 3 months to max 1 g 12 hourly

regimen.adultDose: 2.5-10 mg daily (max 15 mg/day, additional 5 mg evening if needed)

regimen.note: Preferred in renal impairment and elderly

insulinAddition.note: Refer to specialist for combination therapy

referralCriteria: All type 2 at diagnosis and type 1 after 5 years to eye specialist for retinopathy screening

title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
definition: Blood glucose >18 mmol/L, ketones in blood/urine, dehydration, electrolyte imbalance (especially low potassium)
causes: Stress of intercurrent illness (infection, MI, stroke, surgery, complicated pregnancy)
symptoms: Symptoms of infection or underlying condition
signs: Dehydration (dry skin, reduced turgor, sunken eyes)
Evidence of infection, recent surgery, stroke

investigations.name: Blood urea and electrolytes
investigations.purpose: Usually low potassium; high in renal failure

initiatingManagement.rate: 1st litre over 30 min, 2nd over 1 hour, 3rd over 4 hours, 4th over 4 hours, then 1 litre every 6 hours

initiatingManagement.dose: 10-20 units stat, then IV infusion 0.1 units/kg/hour or 5-10 units IM hourly until blood glucose <11 mmol/L

initiatingManagement.timing: Start 2 hours after insulin and saline, ensure urine output >30 ml/hour
initiatingManagement.dose: 10-20 mmol in 500 ml 0.9% saline over ≥1 hour

regularManagement.transition: When eating normally, urine ketones negative/trace, change from sliding scale to twice daily subcutaneous intermediate-acting or premixed insulin

initiatingManagement.rate: 1st hour: 15 ml/kg, 2nd hour: 15 ml/kg, 3rd hour: 7.5 ml/kg, then adjust to requirements

initiatingManagement.dose: 0.15 units/kg stat, then 0.1 units/kg/hour until blood glucose <11 mmol/L
0.2-0.4 mmol/kg (max 10 mmol) in IV fluids over ≥1 hour

slidingScaleExample.note: Sliding scale is patient-specific; doses may need adjustment

adjunct.treatment: Broad-spectrum antibiotics as per appropriate section

adult.potassium: Check 2 hours after starting replacement, then twice daily

referralCriteria: Inadequate resources for management - start 0.9% saline and initial insulin, then refer
Comatose or failing to pass adequate urine despite management

title: Diabetes in Pregnancy

gdrgCodes.description: Expectant management/Medical Treatment in Obstetrics and Gynaecology

definition: Impaired glucose tolerance developing or first recognized during current pregnancy
symptoms: Previous history of large babies (>4 kg)
Previous poor obstetric history (foetal deaths, miscarriages)

investigations.name: Oral glucose tolerance test
investigations.indication: Urine glucose 1+/2+ on 2 occasions or 3+/4+ once

investigations.name: Blood glucose profile
investigations.frequency: Monthly in lab; more frequently by self-monitoring

objectives: Achieve normal blood glucose and HbA1c throughout pregnancy, labour, delivery, puerperium
nonPharmacological: Delivery: by 40 weeks (well controlled), by 38 weeks (insulin-treated, complications)
Caesarean section for severe pre-eclampsia, previous CS, advanced age, malpresentation, foetal macrosomia

pharmacological.preConception: Optimise glycaemic control in known diabetics before pregnancy

absoluteIndicationsForInsulin: Significant obstetric morbidities (foetal macrosomia, polyhydramnios, IUGR)
Poor glycaemic control on oral agents

drugs.dose: 2.5-5 mg 12-24 hourly, increase by 5 mg if needed to max 15 mg/day
drugs.note: Use in 2nd and 3rd trimester only; risk of neonatal hypoglycaemia

insulin.startingDose: 6-10 units total daily of NPH or premixed insulin
insulin.distribution: ⅔ before breakfast, ⅓ before dinner
insulin.adjustment: ±2 units based on blood glucose results
insulin.monitoring: FBS and 2h postprandial every 2-4 weeks until 34 weeks, then weekly

title: Treatment-Induced Hypoglycaemia
definition: Blood glucose <3.6 mmol/L; severe if <2.2 mmol/L

objectives: Rapidly restore blood glucose to normal
Maintain normal levels until eating normally

mild: 2-3 teaspoons granulated sugar or 3 cubes of sugar
Glass of milk or fruit drink
Follow immediately by meal or snack
Repeat after 10 minutes if no improvement

firstLine.adultDose: 50% solution 25-50 ml over 1-3 minutes through large vein, then 5-10% solution 500 ml 4 hourly until eating normally
firstLine.childDose: 10% solution 4 ml/kg over 1-3 minutes, then 5% solution according to daily fluid requirement

title: Dyslipidaemia
causes: Metabolic syndrome (obesity, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, gout)
symptoms: Abdominal pain from pancreatitis related to hypertriglyceridaemia

objectives: Reduce risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular events and deaths in:
- Individuals with previous CVD event (secondary prevention)
nonPharmacological: Low calorie, low saturated fat (animal fat), high polyunsaturated fat (plant fat) diet

referralCriteria: Outside target values despite adequate dietary, exercise and medication therapy

title: Goitre
symptoms: Breathing and swallowing difficulty if large

investigations.name: Thyroid function tests (free T3, free T4, TSH)

objectives: Assess and correct thyroid hormone levels
Identify thyroid neoplasms and manage appropriately

referralCriteria: Full assessment and investigations not possible

title: Hypothyroidism

neonate: Cretinism: mental subnormality, short stature, large tongue, dry skin, sparse hair, protuberant abdomen, umbilical hernia, abnormal facies

investigations.name: Thyroid function tests (free T3, free T4, TSH)

nonPharmacological: Surgical intervention for pituitary lesions where necessary

pharmacological.note: Start low dose especially in elderly and children with heart disease (25-50 mcg), adjust every 6-8 weeks until TSH normal. Treatment is often life-long.

referralCriteria: All ages, especially children with intellectual impairment

title: Hyperthyroidism
signs: Wide pulse pressure (high systolic, low diastolic, e.g. 170/50)
Goitre (smooth diffuse in Graves', irregular in toxic multi-nodular)

investigations.name: Thyroid function tests (free T3, free T4, TSH)

objectives: Reduce thyroid hormone levels to normal

childDose.3-18y: 15 mg daily, adjust based on thyroid hormone level

firstLine.note: Decrease dose when TFTs normal, adjust every 2 months

secondLine.note: Preferred in pregnancy and those who don't tolerate carbimazole

adjunct.note: For symptom control until thyroid function normalises

referralCriteria: Not responding to conventional treatment - secondary/tertiary hospital

title: Adrenal Insufficiency
causes: Sudden cessation of prolonged corticosteroid use/abuse
Stress (infection, trauma, surgery, dental) in undiagnosed adrenal insufficiency or on prolonged corticosteroids
Auto-immune disease of adrenal gland (Addison's disease)
Severe infections affecting adrenal gland (meningococcus, TB, HIV)
signs: Darkening of oral mucosa, gums, skin, palms, soles (some individuals)
Evidence of corticosteroid abuse (skin bleaching, Cushingoid appearance)
Ambiguous genitalia, short stature, failure to thrive (children)

objectives: Identify cause and treat precipitating factor

fluids.childDose: 0.45% Sodium Chloride in 5% Glucose according to fluid requirement

hydrocortisone.adultDose: 200 mg stat, then 100 mg 6 hourly until stable
hydrocortisone.note: May be required for several days. Do not rush to change to maintenance.

corticosteroidAbuse.taper: Gradually over several months (e.g. 2.5 mg/month) and eventually discontinue

adjunct.treatInfection: If present or suspected, appropriate antibiotics

patientEducation: Patients on corticosteroids should report to hospital if ill
Tell doctor, dentist, nurse, pharmacist about corticosteroid use
DO NOT stop treatment if ill, have infection, or undergoing dental procedure - DOUBLE the dose
Revert to IV hydrocortisone for minor surgical procedures including labour and delivery
Reduce dose gradually if treatment >3 weeks and to be stopped
Discourage abuse of oral or topical corticosteroids
referralCriteria: All suspected cases after resuscitation for assessment and long-term management

title: Cushing's Syndrome
causes: Prolonged and excessive intake or abuse of corticosteroids (prednisolone, dexamethasone, hydrocortisone, cortisone)
signs: Easy bruising and bleeding after venepuncture
Inability to rise from squatting position

nonPharmacological: Pituitary or adrenal surgery if tumour diagnosed

pharmacological.note: Treatment dependent on cause, requires specialized investigations

steroidWithdrawalWarning.note: Do NOT withdraw corticosteroids suddenly in Cushing's syndrome due to corticosteroid abuse or prolonged use
steroidWithdrawalWarning.taper: Taper off slowly over several weeks to months to prevent acute adrenal insufficiency

referralCriteria: All suspected cases to endocrinologist/specialist physician for investigations and management

title: Overweight and Obesity

objectives: Loss of 10% initial body weight within 6 months
Rate of loss ≤2-4 kg per month
Achieve ideal BMI and/or abdominal girth
nonPharmacological: Weight reduction diet under dietician supervision
Regular physical activity: 30 minutes brisk walking or equivalent ≥3 days/week if no contraindications
Appropriate management of associated co-morbid disorders

pharmacological.note: Approved anti-obesity treatments available only under specialist guidance

referralCriteria: Severe and morbid obesity - physician specialist
Rapid weight gain over short period - endocrinologist

title: Antenatal Care
principles: Focused Antenatal Care - emphasizes quality over quantity (4 comprehensive visits for normal pregnancy), individualized care, disease detection instead of risk categorization

visitSchedule.booking: As soon as pregnancy suspected (preferably before 14 weeks)
visitSchedule.4th: 36 weeks (return if not delivered within 2 weeks of EDD)

examination: Head to toe and all body systems

followUpAssessment: Ask about health status since last visit
Test urine for albumin and sugar
Examine for weight gain, anaemia, pre-eclampsia

investigations.name: Ultrasound scan
investigations.timing: Early (age, exclude ectopic), 18-24 weeks (anomaly), 36 weeks (placenta position)

objectives: Ensure good health throughout pregnancy, labour, delivery, puerperium
Detect and treat existing/potential health problems
nonPharmacological: - Avoid smoking, alcohol, non-prescribed medications, herbs
- Exercise (moderate, non-weight bearing, avoid supine after 1st trimester)
Develop birth preparedness and complication readiness plan

anaemiaPrevention.note: Combination prenatal formulations acceptable if iron content ≥65 mg/day

malariaProphylaxis.timing: 5 doses, one month apart starting from week 16 or 1st quickening (not beyond week 36)

schedule.TT2: 1 month after TT1 (if no prior immunisation)
schedule.TT3: 6 months after TT2 or subsequent pregnancy
schedule.TT5: Subsequent pregnancy (total 5 doses, no further needed)

highRiskPregnancies: Past history of PPH or retained placenta
Contracted pelvis (height <154 cm, shoe size <4½ UK)
Big baby (SFH >39-40 cm at term, EFW ≥4 kg)
Past stillbirths or early neonatal deaths
Uterine size larger than gestational age
Rhesus negative with Rhesus positive husband

nhisBilling.billingNotes: Maximum 6 antenatal visits per pregnancy covered

title: Hyperemesis Gravidarum

gdrgCodes.description: Expectant management/Medical Treatment in Obstetrics and Gynaecology

definition: Excessive vomiting in early pregnancy, diagnosis of exclusion
causes: May be associated with multiple pregnancy or molar pregnancy
symptoms: Excessive vomiting (inability to keep anything down)
signs: Dehydration (dry skin, dry tongue, sunken eyes)

nonPharmacological: Mild cases: home treatment with frequent small meals
Drink fluids in sips or small volumes

firstLine.note: If body weight <60 kg, give 5 mg 8 hourly. Do not exceed 500 mcg/kg/day

referralCriteria: Severe cases with dehydration, shock, metabolic disturbances

title: Hypertension in Pregnancy

gdrgCodes.description: Expectant management/Medical Treatment in Obstetrics and Gynaecology

definition: Systolic BP ≥140 mmHg and/or diastolic BP ≥90 mmHg on 2 occasions at least 5 minutes apart
referralCriteria: All cases of superimposed pre-eclampsia or other obstetric complications

title: Pre-eclampsia

gdrgCodes.description: Expectant management/Medical Treatment in Obstetrics and Gynaecology

definition: Hypertension and proteinuria in second half of pregnancy
symptoms: Visual disturbances: diplopia, blurred vision, flashes of light (severe)

nonPharmacological: Lie on side to avoid supine hypotension

mild.note: No drug treatment unless BP >150/100 or symptomatic of imminent eclampsia

referralCriteria: All cases to hospital/obstetrician after initiation of treatment

title: Severe Pre-eclampsia and Imminent Eclampsia
definition: Obstetric emergency requiring urgent treatment
symptoms: Visual disturbances (diplopia, blurred vision, flashes of light)

objectives: Stabilise patient and deliver if eclampsia imminent
nonPharmacological: If <34 weeks and not symptomatic, allow pregnancy to continue
If ≥34 weeks, consider delivery after stabilisation

bpManagement.dose: 20 mg stat over ≥1 minute, repeat at 10-min intervals (40 mg, 80 mg, 80 mg) to cumulative 220 mg. When BP <160/110, start infusion 40 mg/hour, double every 30 min to 160 mg/hour.
bpManagement.then: Nifedipine retard 20-40 mg daily or Methyldopa 250-500 mg 8-12 hourly

seizurePrevention.dose: 10 ml of 50% solution (5 g) into each buttock (total 10 g)

referralCriteria: All cases to hospital/obstetrician after initiation of treatment
If foetus immature, transfer to facility capable of looking after immature baby

title: Eclampsia
definition: Complication of worsening pre-eclampsia with convulsions

objectives: Monitor for organ failures and obstetric complications
nonPharmacological: Do NOT put anything in mouth
Maintain airway (hold up chin or mechanical airway)
Turn every hour if unconscious for long period
- Deliver by most appropriate method when stable

fluidReplacement.max: 1 litre in 6 hours or 4 litres in 24 hours
1 litre in 6 hours or 4 litres in 24 hours

convulsionTreatment.dose: 20 ml of 20% solution (4 g) slowly over 5-15 minutes
10 ml of 50% solution (5 g) into each buttock (total 10 g)

recurrentConvulsions.note: If fits recur within 20 minutes, do NOT repeat magnesium sulphate

maintenance.dose: 5 g in alternating buttocks every 4 hours
maintenance.duration: Until 24 hours after last seizure or delivery, whichever later

magnesiumToxicity.presentation: Slowing/arrest of heartbeat and respiration, loss of deep tendon reflexes
precautions: Urine output ≥100 ml over previous 4 hours
Presence of knee jerk/deep tendon reflexes

refractorySeizures.adultDose: 10 mg, then >60kg: 5-10 mg 8 hourly; <60kg: 5 mg 8 hourly

hypertension.dose: 5-10 mg stat over 5-10 min, repeat every 20-30 min, BP not below 140/90
hypertension.infusion: 20-40 mg in 500 ml 0.9% saline, titrate to BP

hypertension.dose: 20 mg stat over ≥1 minute, repeat at 10-min intervals (40 mg, 80 mg, 80 mg) to cumulative 220 mg. When BP <160/110, start infusion 40 mg/hour, double every 30 min to 160 mg/hour.

referralCriteria: Transfer all cases immediately to hospital/obstetrician with operative capacity
Set up IV line of 0.9% saline/Ringer's lactate, give IV magnesium sulphate slowly, follow with IM dose, accompany patient

title: Malaria in Pregnancy

gdrgCodes.description: Expectant management/Medical Treatment in Obstetrics and Gynaecology

note: Refer to D186 (Malaria) for complete details
prevention: Intermittent Preventive Treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) with SP

firstTrimester.note: ACTs not recommended in first trimester unless life-saving

severe.drug: Artesunate IV/IM then ACT for 3 days
severe.alternative: Quinine IV/IM then Quinine + Clindamycin oral

iptp.timing: At each ANC visit after quickening (≥16 weeks)
iptp.note: Delay folic acid supplementation 1 week after SP administration

title: Anaemia in Pregnancy

gdrgCodes.description: Expectant management/Medical Treatment in Obstetrics and Gynaecology

definition: Hb <11 g/dL in pregnancy; severe if <7 g/dL
causes: Poor dietary intake of iron, folate, vitamin B12
signs: Jaundice (may or may not be present)
Hepatosplenomegaly (may or may not be present)

nonPharmacological: Iron-rich foods: red meat, poultry, fish, dark leafy vegetables, shellfish, dried fruits
Avoid iron inhibitors: tea, coffee, bran, cola, eating clay
Folate and vitamin-rich foods: beans, avocado, citrus, spinach, mangoes

bloodTransfusion.note: In labour, severe anaemia best treated by transfusion
bloodTransfusion.withHeartFailure: Transfuse packed cells, add Furosemide 20-40 mg IV if giving whole blood

referralCriteria: Severe anaemia (Hb <7 g/dL) to facility with blood transfusion

title: Sickle Cell Disease in Pregnancy

gdrgCodes.description: Expectant management/Medical Treatment in Obstetrics and Gynaecology

prePregnancyCare: Educate on risks and health problems

booking: Assess risk of frequent crises, previous obstetric complications
Examine nutritional status, anaemia, jaundice, hepatosplenomegaly
Ultrasound to date pregnancy, exclude foetal abnormality, growth retardation

followUp.lowRisk: Monthly until 2nd trimester, then 2 weekly until 28 weeks, then weekly

investigations.name: RDT or blood film for malaria

labourDelivery.setting: Hospital with experienced midwife and obstetrician
preparations: Group and cross-match unit of blood on standby
labourDelivery.mode: Vaginal delivery unless obstetric reasons for CS
labourDelivery.secondStage: Assisted delivery (vacuum) to avoid prolonged second stage
labourDelivery.thirdStage: Active Management of Third Stage of Labour (AMTSL) to prevent PPH

postpartumCare: Hospitalize and monitor closely for at least 72 hours
Check for pallor, puerperal sepsis, acute chest syndrome, vaso-occlusive crises, UTI
Continue prophylactic antibiotics for at least 72 hours
Counsel to avoid crises triggers (stress, pain, lack of sleep, fatigue)
Review at 7 days, then weekly until 6 weeks
Refer to physician for continuing care
Counsel on family planning and offer contraception

management: Admit all sickle cell patients with sickling/obstetric complications
Opioids with care during labour (respiratory depression in baby)
Identify and treat triggers (UTI, malaria, dehydration)
Blood transfusion if Hb <7 g/dL at 36 weeks and/or during crises
Vaso-occlusive crises: maintain hydration with IV fluids 2-4 L/24h, provide analgesia
Acute chest syndrome: foetal monitoring essential

pharmacological.antenatal: As per D110 (Antenatal Care) plus multivitamin once daily

labourPuerperium.dose: 1.2 g stat, then oral 625 mg 12 hourly

referralCriteria: All with obstetric complications to specialist

title: Diabetes Mellitus in Pregnancy

gdrgCodes.description: Expectant management/Medical Treatment in Obstetrics and Gynaecology

note: Refer to D101 (Diabetes in Pregnancy) for complete details

title: Cardiac Disease in Pregnancy

gdrgCodes.description: Expectant management/Medical Treatment in Obstetrics and Gynaecology

signs: Signs of cardiac disease depending on lesion

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.note: With foetal protection

objectives: Maintain good cardiac function throughout pregnancy, labour, delivery, puerperium

referralCriteria: All patients to specialist physician/cardiologist and obstetrician

title: Jaundice in Pregnancy

gdrgCodes.description: Expectant management/Medical Treatment in Obstetrics and Gynaecology

nonObstetric: Haemolytic: malaria, sickle cell, G6PD, septicaemia, drugs, herbs
Surgical: acute cholecystitis, cholelithiasis, obstructive jaundice

signs: Yellow discoloration of mucous membranes and skin
Signs relating to cause (fever, elevated BP)

investigations.name: FBC, blood film for malaria, RDT

treatment.pharmacological: Depends on underlying cause (see appropriate sections)

referralCriteria: Severe cases and those with abdominal pain to specialist

title: Post-Partum Haemorrhage

types.primary: Bleeding >500 ml within 24 hours of delivery, or any amount causing haemodynamic compromise
types.secondary: Excessive vaginal bleeding from 24 hours to 6 weeks after delivery

causes.retainedPlacenta: All or part of placental tissue

symptoms: Excessive or prolonged vaginal bleeding after delivery

retainedPlacenta: If incomplete, explore uterus under anaesthesia
wellContractedUterus: Examine in lithotomy position with adequate analgesia/anaesthesia
nonPharmacological.coagulopathy: Bedside clotting test - 5 ml blood in 10 ml tube should clot in 6 minutes

firstLine.infusion: 10-40 units in 500 ml Dextrose saline or 0.9% saline

secondLine.dose: 600 mcg for PPH prophylaxis within 1 min delivery, 800 mcg for PPH treatment

options.note: Ergometrine not for hypertension or heart disease

protractedBleeding.dose: 1 g stat, then 1 g 8 hourly (max 3 g/24h)

hypovolaemia.fluid: Sodium Chloride 0.9% or Ringer's lactate IV

ifNoAnaesthetist.note: Do NOT mix with pethidine. Monitor respiratory rate, stop if <12/min
Use only by trained Medical Officer/anaesthetist

referralCriteria: To hospital with theatre and blood transfusion facilities

title: Post-Partum Pyrexia

gdrgCodes.description: Expectant management/Medical Treatment in Obstetrics and Gynaecology

definition: Body temperature ≥38°C on 2 or more occasions during first 10 days of puerperium excluding first day

nonPharmacological: Examination under anaesthesia with possible uterine curettage for retained products
Encourage frequent emptying of breasts if engorgement
Incision and drainage for breast/perianal abscess

gdrgCodes.description: Spontaneous Vaginal Delivery with or without Episiotomy

regimen.plus: Promethazine 25 mg IV/IM as required to reduce vomiting and potentiate analgesia
regimen.note: Maximum effect 45 min, lasts 3-4 hours. Best not to give if delivery within 4 hours (up to 6-7 cm dilatation)

firstStageInhalational.timing: Late first stage, delivery expected within 1 hour

noAnaesthetist.note: Monitor respiratory rate, stop if <10/min. Do NOT mix drugs in same syringe

dose.IV infusion: 1 mg per ml in 5% dextrose or saline, maintenance 10-45 mcg/kg/min

neonatalRespiratoryDepression.cause: Baby born within 6 hours of pethidine

treatment.dose: 100 mcg/kg stat, repeat 100 mcg after 3-8 minutes if no response
treatment.support: Resuscitation with oxygen via facemask/ETT and self-inflating bag

title: Preterm Labour in Premature Delivery

gdrgCodes.description: Expectant management/Medical Treatment in Obstetrics and Gynaecology

definition: Labour occurring after 28 weeks but before 37 completed weeks
symptoms: Regular and painful uterine contractions or abdominal pains
signs: Progressive effacement and dilatation of cervix

investigations.name: Ultrasound scan
investigations.purpose: Gestational age, lie, presentation, amniotic fluid volume, placental site, foetal weight

Allow foetal growth and maturation in utero if feasible
Allow labour to progress if already well established

foetalLungMaturation.effectiveness: Most effective if delivery ≥24 hours after first dose and <7 days after last dose
foetalLungMaturation.caution: Avoid if infection present. Risks: susceptibility to infection, fluid retention, pulmonary oedema, maternal postpartum collapse

gdrgCodes.description: Expectant management/Medical Treatment in Obstetrics and Gynaecology

Uterine size smaller than gestational age
If chorioamnionitis: fever, purulent discharge, foetal tachycardia, maternal tachycardia, uterine tenderness

Ultrasound scan
investigations.purpose: Gestational age, lie, presentation, amniotic fluid volume, placental site, foetal weight

objectives: Prevent labour if preterm and baby immature
Improve foetal survival through lung maturity

title: Dysmenorrhoea

gdrgCodes.description: Expectant management/Medical Treatment in Obstetrics and Gynaecology

symptoms: Lower abdominal pain (cramping/colicky, may be dull constant)
Pain may radiate to lower back or legs
Nausea, vomiting, headaches, dizziness (may be associated)

hormonal.note: For women requiring contraception and severe dysmenorrhoea
For women with heavy bleeding and dysmenorrhoea

title: Abortion

gdrgCodes.description: Expectant management/Medical Treatment in Obstetrics and Gynaecology

definition: Expulsion of foetus and products of conception before 28th week of pregnancy

spontaneous: Chronic illness (diabetes, thyroid disorders, sickle cell)

symptoms: Scanty to moderate painless vaginal bleeding
signs: Uterine size compatible with gestational age

Start one medication at a time, at lowest possible dose

Flaky skin rash especially on legs

title: Anaemia
symptoms: Pica (craving for ice, dirt, clay - iron deficiency)

investigations.name: Full Blood Count
investigations.purpose: Hb, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RBC indices

nonPharmacological: Regular intake of leafy vegetables, fresh fruits, beans, liver, meat, eggs, fish
High fibre diets to reduce bleeding from haemorrhoids
Surgical treatment where applicable (haemorrhoids, tumours, hypersplenism)

vitaminB12Deficiency.childDose: 1 mg stat, then 1 mg every 3 months for life

title: Bleeding Disorders
causes: Drug-induced (herbal preparations, aspirin, clopidogrel, warfarin, heparin)
symptoms: Spontaneous bleeding from mucous membranes or cuts
Excessive bleeding from cuts or incisions
Deformed joints from recurrent joint bleeds (haemophilia)
Swelling at site of blood collection (pseudotumours)
signs: Localised swelling due to bleeding into body spaces (joints, muscles)
Limitation of movement and joint deformities

nonPharmacological: Apply regulated pressure dressing and/or ice packs to minimise bleeding
Stop any drugs responsible for bleeding
Avoid unnecessary injections and surgical procedures

fluidReplacement.blood: Concentrated red cell transfusion 3 ml/kg for each expected 1 g/dL haemoglobin rise

title: Sickle Cell Disease

gdrgCodes.description: Sickle Cell Disease with Complication/Crisis ≥12 Yrs
Sickle Cell Disease with Complication/Crisis <12 Yrs

causes: Inheritance of two abnormal haemoglobin genes from both parents, at least one of which is S
Crisis precipitants: cold weather, dehydration, infection, physical exertion, mental stress

vasoOcclusiveCrisis: Joint and bone pain (especially during cold wet seasons)
Difficulty in breathing with or without chest pain
Priapism (spontaneous sustained erection without sexual arousal)

signs: Splenomegaly (may be absent in older patients due to auto-infarction)
Growth delay or tall, lanky stature ('marfanoid' habitus)

investigations.name: Blood Film Comment
investigations.purpose: Sickle cells, target cells, Howell-Jolly bodies

objectives: Prevent the development of sickle cell crises
Maintain a good steady state haemoglobin
Prevent long-term complications and organ damage
Manage sickle cell crises and complications
nonPharmacological: Good hydration at all times (adequate water/fluids)
Avoid precipitating causes (malaria, dehydration, stress, excessive exercise, extremes of weather)
Genetic counselling with voluntary family size restriction

mildToModeratePain.note: If pain not controlled within 12 hours, consult specialist

acuteChestSyndrome.note: Urgent chest X-ray, blood and urine cultures, blood grouping and cross-matching

title: Plasma Cell Myeloma
causes: Potential precipitants: chemicals (dioxins, formaldehyde, nitrobenzene), ionizing radiation, viruses (HHV8, EBV, HIV, hepatitis viruses)

investigations.name: Skeletal Survey

investigations.purpose: Elevated IgG, IgA, or light chains

objectives: Reduce the number of abnormal plasma cells
nonPharmacological: Drink at least 3 litres of fluid daily throughout the course of disease

renalComplications.treatment: IV and oral fluids, maintain urine output >3 L/day

treatment.note: Zoledronic acid or pamidronate under specialist care

referralCriteria: All patients suspected to have plasma cell myeloma should be referred to a haematologist at a specialised tertiary centre

nhisBilling.typicalLOS: 5-14 days for initial diagnosis and complications
